title: Slices 🍕
description: A special kind of reference to a sequence of elements.

slides.title: A Slice of Life 🍕
slides.content:
A **Slice** is exactly what it sounds like. It lets you reference a *contiguous sequence* of elements in a collection, rather than the whole collection at once.

A slice is a type of reference, so it strictly **does not have ownership**.

slides.title: String Slices 🧶

quizData.question: Does a string slice (`&str`) take ownership of the data?

options.text: Yes, it creates a new copy
No, because a slice is just an immutable reference/pointer to a portion of the original String
Yes, but only the first 5 letters

quizData.explanation: Slices just 'borrow' the string and point to specific start/end locations. They never own data.

slides.title: The Infinite Power of &str 🔌
slides.content:
We've been secretly using slices all along!

When we write string literals directly into the code like `let text = "Hi!";`...
The type of `text` is strictly `&str`. It’s a slice pointing directly to that specific location of the binary execution file!

quizData.question: What is the data type of the hardcoded literal code `let s = "Hello";`?
quizData.explanation: String literals compiled directly into the executable are always immutable string slices `&str`.
